episode_meta.title: Neil Brown: The Pulitzer Prizes, AI Transparency, and Journalism's Next Evolution

ai_tools: satellite imagery and computer-assisted reporting tools (referenced in BuzzFeed/NYT Uyghur investigation)
generative AI tools (unspecified, disclosed by Pulitzer Prize entrants)

use_cases.description: City Bureau and Invisible Institute used AI in producing 'Missing in Chicago,' a Pulitzer Prize-winning investigative entry that openly disclosed AI use in the submission questionnaire
use_cases.outcome: Won a Pulitzer Prize; one of the first two winners to openly acknowledge AI use

use_cases.description: The New York Times visual investigations desk used AI to assist their Pulitzer Prize-winning entry
use_cases.outcome: Won a Pulitzer Prize; one of the first two winners to openly acknowledge AI use

use_cases.description: BuzzFeed and the New York Times used satellite imagery and computer-assisted reporting to map likely Uyghur detention centers across China, identifying far more facilities than the handful China had acknowledged
use_cases.tool_used: Satellite imagery and computer-assisted reporting tools
use_cases.outcome: Won a Pulitzer Prize (circa 2020-2021); revealed extent of Uyghur detention centers across China that were previously unconfirmed

use_cases.description: Neil Brown personally uses ChatGPT to draft remarks and speeches, then edits the output, finding it 'amazingly sophisticated, cool, and weak all at the same time'
use_cases.outcome: Reinforced the value of human editing; output deemed not good enough without editorial refinement

use_cases.description: Poynter Institute staff use Fireflies for meeting transcription
use_cases.outcome: Used as standard meeting note-taking tool

use_cases.description: Poynter Institute encourages staff to experiment with ChatGPT and Perplexity as part of organizational learning
use_cases.outcome: Institutional policy of encouraging experimentation; recently updated AI ethics guidelines

key_claims.claim: Five Pulitzer Prize finalists acknowledged using some form of generative AI, and two of them won, but we don't know if they were the first to use AI -- only the first to be asked about it via the new disclosure questionnaire
key_claims.speaker: Neil Brown

key_claims.claim: The Pulitzer Board never previously asked about other technological tools like computer-assisted reporting, data visualization software, or specific gathering techniques, so the AI disclosure requirement may reflect catching up rather than something brand new
key_claims.speaker: Neil Brown

key_claims.claim: AI use was not disqualifying for the Pulitzer Prizes; the board made no moral judgment about AI and focused on the totality of the journalism produced
key_claims.speaker: Neil Brown

key_claims.claim: Technology has been advancing journalism for decades; AI is the latest evolution, not a fundamentally different category
key_claims.speaker: Neil Brown

key_claims.claim: It's 2025 and over 60% of newsroom staff in some organizations have never even tried a tool like ChatGPT
key_claims.speaker: Nikita Roy

key_claims.claim: The World Economic Forum's 2025 Future of Jobs report projects 92 million jobs displaced but 170 million new jobs created -- a net positive of 78 million jobs
key_claims.speaker: Nikita Roy

key_claims.claim: Technology does not erase inaccuracy, much as it doesn't automatically cause it either; imperfection is part of the equation regardless of how powerful the tools are
key_claims.speaker: Neil Brown

key_claims.claim: If a machine or technology distills content into something that is not ultimately distinctive or distinguished, it probably won't make the cut for a Pulitzer Prize
key_claims.speaker: Neil Brown

key_claims.claim: Creativity and technology should not be put at war with each other; there's been great journalism with amazing reporting but weak writing, and vice versa
key_claims.speaker: Neil Brown

challenges.description: The discussion around AI in journalism remains overly binary -- framed as simply 'good' or 'bad' -- rather than nuanced consideration of specific use cases and audience value
Newsrooms struggle with the tension between AI's efficiency gains and the displacement of existing jobs and roles, creating labor challenges that are 'really difficult and tricky'
Many newsrooms are slow to adopt and experiment with AI tools; over 60% of some newsrooms have never tried ChatGPT, risking falling behind as tech companies evolve information distribution

AI-generated content may lack the distinctiveness and creative quality that distinguishes high-end journalism and writing, raising questions about whether machine-assembled work constitutes genuine 'writing'
Newsrooms tend to evaluate AI experiments over too-short timeframes (3-6 months) rather than taking a longitudinal approach, leading to premature abandonment of promising tools
Maintaining accuracy remains fundamental; AI tools like ChatGPT can produce inaccurate or incomplete results, just as human reporters can transpose facts or misinterpret data
Ensuring transparency and disclosure about AI use without creating a false impression that AI is uniquely problematic compared to other technological tools that were never disclosed
The journalism industry historically puts its head down and hopes disruption goes away, as happened during the print-to-digital transition, and risks repeating this pattern with AI
Newsrooms are siloed between technologists and journalists rather than blending these skills; technologists and journalists need to understand each other's domains
Newsrooms do not engage their audiences enough in AI product development and adoption, leaving them to catch up after audience behavior has already shifted

quotes.text: We didn't say AI was disqualifying in any fashion, and as you can tell from the two that won, it wasn't. But we also didn't linger on essentially a moral judgment about it. We just really believed that transparency is a value we should live by.
quotes.speaker: Neil Brown
quotes.topic: Pulitzer Board's approach to AI disclosure

quotes.text: I want us to not put creativity and technology at war with each other.
quotes.speaker: Neil Brown
quotes.topic: False choice between creativity and technology

quotes.text: I actually, I hope this doesn't come across as blame, mostly as a party game. You know, if I have to make remarks or something like that, I like to go in, see what it says. And it reminds me, it's amazingly sophisticated, cool, and weak all at the same time.
quotes.speaker: Neil Brown
quotes.topic: Personal experience with ChatGPT revealing its limitations

quotes.text: The number one mistake to avoid doing again is to put your head down and hope it goes away. I do think that there was a little bit of that, maybe not a little bit, maybe a lot of that, as we moved to online digital journalism.
quotes.speaker: Neil Brown
quotes.topic: Key lesson from print-to-digital transition applicable to AI

quotes.text: Newsrooms today, in my opinion, need to be a much more comfortable blend of technologists and journalists. And by the way, technologists need to know a fair bit about journalism and journalists need to know a fair bit about the technology.
quotes.speaker: Neil Brown

quotes.text: I've had the best luck when the technologists first ask 'what are you trying to do?' Not 'do I have the tool for it.'
quotes.speaker: Neil Brown
quotes.topic: Effective collaboration between technologists and journalists

recommendations.advice: News organizations should require AI disclosure in journalism submissions and practices, not as a moral judgment but as an extension of the fundamental journalistic value of transparency
recommendations.speaker: Neil Brown

recommendations.advice: Every journalist and news professional should personally experiment with AI tools like ChatGPT rather than forming opinions about tools they've never tried -- that's bad reporting
recommendations.speaker: Neil Brown

recommendations.advice: Don't repeat the print-to-digital mistake of putting your head down and hoping disruption goes away; embrace risk-taking and give staff time on the job to learn how AI tools work
recommendations.speaker: Neil Brown

recommendations.advice: Engage audiences early in AI product development rather than waiting until after you've figured out what the tools can do; drive down the road together with your audience rather than making them wait for you to catch up
recommendations.speaker: Neil Brown

recommendations.advice: Take a longitudinal approach to AI investments -- collect lessons learned and best practices over time, share them across the industry, and give experiments more than just 3-6 months before making judgments
recommendations.speaker: Neil Brown

recommendations.advice: Focus on retraining and repurposing existing staff rather than viewing AI as purely a displacement threat; fundamentals remain valuable even as form changes, and people can adapt their skills to modern uses
recommendations.speaker: Neil Brown

recommendations.advice: Start with the mission of the work when evaluating AI's role: if the mission is creative endeavor, a large language model may not meet the creative standard; if the mission is gathering information at speed, AI may excel
recommendations.speaker: Neil Brown

recommendations.advice: Foster a culture where technologists start by asking journalists 'what are you trying to do?' and journalists learn enough tech literacy to have productive dialogue with technologists
recommendations.speaker: Neil Brown

recommendations.advice: The journalism industry needs to do more reporting on itself -- understanding how audiences consume information and where they're heading, rather than reacting after the fact
recommendations.speaker: Neil Brown

external_references: Pulitzer Prizes (AI disclosure questionnaire added to entry process)

City Bureau and Invisible Institute ('Missing in Chicago' Pulitzer winner)
New York Times visual investigations desk (Pulitzer winner)
BuzzFeed (Pulitzer Prize-winning Uyghur detention center investigation, circa 2020-2021)
